dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 3 to Shafer St/Broad St for breathing problems, reported with no apparent distress.
Hello, my name is Mark. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've had trouble breathing for a while, and it's gotten worse.
Can you open your eyes without prompting?
GCS is 15, and he's alert and oriented to time, person, place, and purpose.
Could you tell me where you are right now?
Can you point to your left hand for me?
Your airway is open and clear with no signs of obstruction.
Breathing is rapid at 23 per minute and is not deep.

dialogue.micro_intent: Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Oxygen saturation is 96% with supplemental oxygen.
Pulse is 116, fast but strong. 12. Primary Assessment; Skin appearance: Color; EMT: Skin is pink with no indications of discoloration.
The skin feels warm to the touch.
Current blood pressure is 167/76.
No active bleeding present.
Can you tell me more about your breathing and the other symptoms?
It's shortness of breath most of the time, and I've had some diarrhea.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Can you tell me if something in the past might relate to this issue?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I was shot in the head eight years ago; since then, I've had breathing problems.
Do you have any known allergies?
No known drug or food allergies.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Prednisone, Albuterol, budesonide, Diltiazem, gabapentin, Loperamide, quetiapine, trazodone, and aspirin.
Let me confirm your medical history.
I have Primary Hypertension, COPD, hyperlipidemia, and a history of alcohol use.
Was the last time you ate or drank recently?
I think it was a few hours ago.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: You mentioned housing concerns. Is that correct?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Yes, I am homeless.
Are you currently experiencing any chest pain or discomfort?
No actual pain, but I do feel short of breath.
For how long has this been happening?
It's been on and off for a while, and it's gotten progressively worse. 34. Secondary Assessment; Skin exam - Color: Jaundice, pallor, etc.; EMT: No jaundice or pallor is noted, skin tone is normal.
Skin is dry and warm to the touch.
Skin turgor is normal-pins up quickly.
Head is normal in shape with no deformities.

dialogue.micro_intent: Head and neck exam - jugular venous distention
dialogue.utterance: No jugular venous distention is noted.
Let me listen to your lungs again for clearness.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Abdomen is soft and not distended.
Bowel sounds are not auscultated for today's encounter.
Pelvis is normal with no signs of tenderness or instability.
No visible rectal bleeding or abnormal coloration in urine is observed.

dialogue.micro_intent: Extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Let me know if you have any trouble moving your arms and legs.

dialogue.micro_intent: Extremities and back exam - Vertebral column
dialogue.utterance: I can move them fine, no pain in the back or pelvis.

dialogue.micro_intent: Extremities and back exam - Peripheral pulses
dialogue.utterance: Peripheral pulses are strong and regular on both sides.
Respiration is now very rapid, 36 per minute.
Blood pressure is 147/109.
Pulse decreased to 107 but still fast.
Oxygen saturation is still at 96% with supplemental oxygen.
EKG initially was not recorded, now shows sinus tachycardia with a short PR interval.
Glucose levels remain stable and unchanged.
I just activated the 4-lead ECG for ongoing monitoring.
I'm starting low-flow oxygen at 2 LPM.
I've obtained a 12-lead EKG. I'll send it to the receiving hospital per protocol.
Let's attempt an IV into an extremity vein for medication access.
First IV placement attempt was unsuccessful. I'll prepare the equipment for another site.
Based on the response and findings, we're continuing under Protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress.
Would you be comfortable going to Retreat Doctors' Hospital?
Yes, I'll go there.
I'll help you onto the stretcher. We'll do it gently.
Be gentle-my breathing worsens with sudden movement.
Chest is symmetric and atraumatic with no signs of tracheal deviation.
Please raise your arms if you feel discomfort at any time.
He's secure now, and the transport will be smooth.
The failed IV site is clean, and skin turgor appears normal before transport.
He's alert and cooperative during assessment and transport.
I'll make sure the ED receives the full profile of medications and vitals.
Patient discloses a history of rural shelter living with no stable address at present.
We'll transport you safely and monitor your vitals closely during the ride.

dialogue.micro_intent: Head and neck exam - tracheal deviation
dialogue.utterance: Trachea is midline with no deviation.
The airway remains patent with no signs of obstruction.
Documented past alcohol intoxication contributes to ongoing medical concerns.
Peripheral pulses are easily felt and regular.
Skin is pink and warm with no distress signs to extremities.
Abdomen is not tender or rigid.
Chest movement is symmetrical and smooth.
Respirations remain 36 per minute, very rapid.
Pulse is 107 and remains bounding.
Manually interpreted as Sinus Tachycardia on 12-lead EKG.
Supplemental oxygen remains unchanged at 2 LPM.
No further IV attempts are pursued at this time.
Patient remains stable during transport.
Transport occurs without incident and patient remains stable.
Patient's age is 57, male, 91 kg, with no marital status noted.
Transport plan and care were executed consistently with Protocol 3-11.
Primary receiving facility was as requested by the patient.
Patient ambulated with assistance to the ED after being transferred from stretcher.
Full report handed off to the Emergency Department triage nurse on arrival.
All handoff information provided to RN. No system failed or interrupted.
Patient transferred into the care of ED staff at Retreat Doctors' Hospital.
No adverse events reported during transfer from scene to hospital.
Patient destinations confirmed at ED Room 3 as noted in the pre-hospital course.
Oxygen and monitoring remain ongoing during transport per RN direction.
